stem: The integrand \( \frac{[[c]]}{\sqrt{x}} \) is unbounded as \( x \to 0^{+} \), but the improper integral still converges. Evaluate \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} \frac{[[c]]}{\sqrt{x}}\,dx \).
solution: \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} [[c]]x^{-1/2}\,dx = \left[2\cdot[[c]]\sqrt{x}\right]_{0}^{[[a]]} = 2\cdot[[c]]\sqrt{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \); the singularity is integrable since the exponent \( -\tfrac12 > -1 \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 from antidifferentiating \( x^{-1/2} \).
This treats the integrand as if it were \( \sqrt{x} \) rather than \( 1/\sqrt{x} \).
This doubles the correct value by an extra factor of 2.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Evaluate the convergent improper integral \( \int_{1}^{\infty} \frac{[[c]]}{x^{2} + [[d]]}\,dx \), where the interval of integration is unbounded but the integrand decays like \( 1/x^{2} \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the arctangent value at the lower limit \( x=1 \).
This doubles the correct value through a factor-of-2 slip in the arctan formula.
This drops the \( \pi/2 \) term from the limit at infinity entirely.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Both limits of integration are infinite. Evaluate \( \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{[[c]]}{x^{2} + [[d]]}\,dx \).
distractor_rationales: This halves the correct value, as if only integrating over the positive half-line.
This multiplies by \( \sqrt{[[d]]} \) instead of dividing by it.
This doubles the correct value.

stem: Although \( \ln x \to -\infty \) as \( x \to 0^{+} \), the improper integral \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} [[c]]\ln x\,dx \) still converges. Evaluate it.
solution: \( \int_{0}^{[[a]]} [[c]]\ln x\,dx = [[c]]\left[x\ln x - x\right]_{0}^{[[a]]} = [[c]]\left([[a]]\ln [[a]] - [[a]]\right) = [[answer]] \), using \( \lim_{x\to0^{+}} x\ln x = 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to subtract the \( -x \) term from the antiderivative.
This drops the \( [[a]] \) factor multiplying the logarithm entirely.
This flips the sign of the \( -[[a]] \) term, adding instead of subtracting.
This doubles the correct value.
This halves the correct value.
This flips the sign of the correct value.
